title: Count Reverse Pairs

content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer array numsnumsnums. Your task is to **count the number of reverse pairs** in the array.

A **reverse pair** is defined as a pair of indices (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that:


`0 <= i < j < nums.length`, and
`nums[i] > 2 * nums[j]`.
Function DeclarationFunction NamereversePairsreversePairsreversePairs – This function counts the number of reverse pairs in the given array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : An integer array of length NNN containing positive, negative, or zero values.
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the **total number of reverse pairs** in the array.
Constraints
1≤T≤1001 \leq T \leq 1001≤T≤100
1≤N≤5×1041 \le N \le 5 \times 10^41≤N≤5×104
−231≤nums[i]≤231−1-2^{31} \le nums[i] \le 2^{31} - 1−231≤nums[i]≤231−1
Time-efficient solutions are expected due to large input size.

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the array elements.




Output Format

For each test case, output a single integer — the number of reverse pairs in the array.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer — the number of reverse pairs in the given array.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case the reverse pairs are (0,1), (0,2) and (0,3). For the second test case the reverse pairs are (0,1), (0,4), (2,3), (2,4) and (3,4).
There are no reverse pairs in the array.

solution.approach: We use a classic divide‑and‑conquer (merge sort) algorithm. While merging two sorted halves we can count all reverse pairs (i, j) with i in the left half and j in the right half because the left part is sorted. For each element in the left half we advance a pointer in the right half until nums[left] <= 2*nums[right]; the number of elements skipped gives the count of valid pairs for that left element. After counting, we perform the normal merge to keep the array sorted for upper recursion levels. This runs in O(N log N) time and O(N) auxiliary space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case, O(total N log N) for all test cases combined.
